summary:
Yes, apelin is generally reduced in diabetes due to its association with obesity-related inflammation and declining production in fatty tissues associated with type 2 diabetes. This reduction reflects impaired metabolic function tied directly to insulin resistance mechanisms observed in diabetic patients. 

Note: Only include information from Agent1’s original reasoning; do not add external knowledge or interpret beyond what was stated.

- In exactly two sentences

From Agent1's reasoning provided:
The core point is about how adipose tissue changes under obesity – shifting from secretions like apelin (anti-inflammatory) to inflammatory cytokines when fat accumulates. It explicitly links this shift to decreasing apelin levels during conditions related to T2D because "apelin levels are noted to decline" with increasing fat mass which correlates strongly with diabetes development through shared pathophysiology involving obesity-driven inflammation.

Final summary must reflect just those elements without adding interpretation or outside context.
